coredata.dc:title: Engineered DNA-copper double-modified NIR carbon nanodots as oxidative stress amplifier for targeted intracellular production of reactive oxygen species
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Ding-Kun
Zhang, Rongjun
Bianco, Alberto
coredata.dc:description: Regulating redox homeostasis is an advanced and efficient approach for cancer treatment. Well-designed catalytic drugs, which can catalytic generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in targeted cells, minimizing side effects, are urgently needed. Here, we developed a near infrared (NIR) carbon dot-based oxidative stress amplifier (named AptCND-Cu) for targeted cellular imaging and intracellular generation of ROS. AptCND-Cu was designed and prepared through a controllable double-covalent modification of NIR CNDs with a DNA aptamer and Cu complex. Detailed characterization techniques, including TEM, AFM, XPS and DLS, were used to confirm the successful coupling of both oligomer aptamer and metal complex. The resulting AptCND-Cu owns superior tumor targeting, stable NIR fluorescence (FL) emission, good photostability, and stable copper delivery. We demonstrate that this material can deplete glutathione (GSH) and can amplify Cu-induced Fenton-like reaction eventually causing cell death in vitro. AptCND-Cu exhibit also long tumor retention time in tumor-bearing mice and enhanced body clearance. This work not only propose an alternative strategy to the multifunctionalization of red-emissive CNDs, by simply integrating DNA and ion-based catalytic drugs, but also broadens their uses in cancer catalytic nanomedicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Carbon dots
Catalytic drugs
Biomaterials
Cancer treatment

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of the N3-(CH2)3–NH2 to CNDs
Detection of GSH and GSSG in solution
Catalytic activity of AptCND-Cu at different pH
Catalytic activity of AptCND-Cu at different temperature

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the concept. a) Structure of NIR CNDs-based oxidative stress amplifier (AptCND-Cu). b) Use of AptCND-Cu for targeted intracellular production of ROS. I) receptor endocytosis, and II) Fenton-like reaction. Upon endocytosis, Cu2+ can react with GSH to produce GSSG and Cu+. Cu + generates reactive hydroxyl radicals from endogenous H2O2. AptCND-Cu nanoparticles can be visualized using fluorescence confocal microscopy benefiting from their stable NIR fluorescence signals.

a) HRTEM of NIR CNDs, N3-CND-DTPA, CND-DTPA-Cu and AptCND-Cu; b) Bright filed (BF) (I) and high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) STEM images of AptCND-Cu (II), along with the corresponding element mapping for copper (III) and merging of HAADF and copper mapping (IIII). c) FT-IR spectra of CNDs, and N3-CND-DTPA (magnified spectra are visible in Fig. S1). d) Zeta potential and e) DLS graphs of CNDs, N3-CND-DTPA, CND-DTPA-Cu and AptCND-Cu at the concentration of 25 μg mL−1 in PBS buffer (pH = 7.4).

a) AFM image and b) the height of AptCND-Cu nanoparticles. c) Agarose gel electrophoresis of Aptmer-DBCO, N3-CND-DTPA-Cu and AptCND-Cu. d) XPS survey spectrum of AptCND-Cu. (e) High resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p.

a) UV–Vis and FL spectra of AptCND-Cu. The insert is a photo of AptCND-Cu at 1 mg mL−1 in PBS buffer. b) Comparison of the photostability of AptCND-Cu and commercial near-infrared dyes Cy5. All the samples were continuously irradiated using a 10 W LED lamp for 100 min. I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of the AptCND-Cu at 620 nm and Cy5 at 650 nm before or after irradiation, respectively. c) Stability of AptCND-Cu in cell culture medium with 10% FBS. d) Leakage of Cu from AptCND-Cu solution during 72 h.

a) Total GSH level (GSH + GSSG) and level of GSSG in the solution of 2.5 μM GSH with or without AptCND-Cu. b) UV/Vis absorption spectra of MB after treatment with or without AptCND-Cu (50 μg mL−1), H2O2 (11 mM), and GSH (8 mM): 1) MB; 2) MB + H2O2; 3) MB + H2O2 + GSH; 4) MB + H2O2 + GSH + AptCND-Cu. c) UV/Vis absorption spectra of MB after incubation with AptCND-Cu (50 μg mL−1), H2O2 (12 mM), and GSH (8 mM) at different temperatures. d) UV/Vis absorption spectra of MB after incubation with AptCND-Cu (50 μg mL−1), H2O2 (11 mM), and GSH (8 mM) at different pH.

Aptamer-mediated targeted internalization of AptCND-Cu in cancer cells. a) and b) Flow cytometry test of HCT 116 cells were treated with AptCND-Cu (100 μg mL−1) with or without aptamer (5 μM and 10 μM, competitive inhibitor). c) Confocal microscopy images showing the targeted uptake of AptCND-Cu (100 μg mL−1) into HCT 116 cells at different time points (4, 8, 24 h). In green, membranes stained with Cell Mask (excitation channel: 488 nm, emission: 500–550 nm); in red, AptCND-Cu (excitation channel: 405 nm, emission: 650–715 nm). The scale bar is 20 μm.

Intracellular ROS generation by AptCND-Cu in cancer cells. Fluorescent images of HCT 116 cells treated with CNDs, AptCND and AptCND-Cu at the concentration of 100 μg mL−1. DCFH-DA was used as the fluorogenic marker for ROS detection (excitation channel: 488 nm, emission: 500–550 nm), The scale bar is 20 μm.

Catalytic therapy efficacy of AptCND-Cu against cancer cells. Fluorescence images of calcein AM/PI-stained HCT 116 cells co-incubated with AptCND-Cu or CNDs. In green, live cells stained with calcein AM (excitation channel: 488 nm, emission: 500–550 nm); in red, dead cells stained with PI (excitation channel: 488 nm, emission: 620–720 nm).

In vivo targeted imaging and biodistribution of AptCND-Cu. a) In vivo fluorescence imaging of HCT 116 tumor bearing mice at various time points. Mice were tail intravenously injected with CNDs and AptCND-Cu (200 μL, 1 mg mL−1). Circles indicate the position of the tumor. b) Fluorescence intensity of tumors at different time points based on in vivo fluorescent images shown in panel a). c) Ex vivo NIR imaging of major organs and tumor extracted at 4 h. d) NIR fluorescence signals of major organs and tumors at 4 h. e) NIR fluorescence signals of tumors at 4 h. f) Ex vivo NIR imaging of major organs and tumor extracted at 24 h. g) NIR fluorescence signals of major organs and tumors at 24 h. h) NIR fluorescence signal of tumors at 24 h.

head.ce:title: Engineered DNA-copper double-modified NIR carbon nanodots as oxidative stress amplifier for targeted intracellular production of reactive oxygen species

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Molecular Medicine (IMM), Renji Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, Shanghai 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Molecular Medicine (IMM), Renji Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, Shanghai 200240, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CNRS, Immunology, Immunopathology and Therapeutic Chemistry, UPR 3572, University of Strasbourg, ISIS, 67000 Strasbourg, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CNRS, Immunology, Immunopathology and Therapeutic Chemistry, UPR 3572, University of Strasbourg, ISIS, 67000 Strasbourg, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Regulating redox homeostasis is an advanced and efficient approach for cancer treatment. Well-designed catalytic drugs, which can catalytic generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in targeted cells, minimizing side effects, are urgently needed. Here, we developed a near infrared (NIR) carbon dot-based oxidative stress amplifier (named AptCND-Cu) for targeted cellular imaging and intracellular generation of ROS. AptCND-Cu was designed and prepared through a controllable double-covalent modification of NIR CNDs with a DNA aptamer and Cu complex. Detailed characterization techniques, including TEM, AFM, XPS and DLS, were used to confirm the successful coupling of both oligomer aptamer and metal complex. The resulting AptCND-Cu owns superior tumor targeting, stable NIR fluorescence (FL) emission, good photostability, and stable copper delivery. We demonstrate that this material can deplete glutathione (GSH) and can amplify Cu-induced Fenton-like reaction eventually causing cell death in vitro. AptCND-Cu exhibit also long tumor retention time in tumor-bearing mice and enhanced body clearance. This work not only propose an alternative strategy to the multifunctionalization of red-emissive CNDs, by simply integrating DNA and ion-based catalytic drugs, but also broadens their uses in cancer catalytic nanomedicine.

ce:section.ce:para: Ding-Kun Ji: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft preparation, Funding acquisition. Rongjun Zhang: Data curation, Visualization. Alberto Bianco: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

Cell homeostasis, guaranteed by the maintenance of a balance between redox species like GSH and H2O2, plays a key role in many biological processes like cell signaling, biosynthesis, cell aging and apoptosis [1,2]. It is well-known that cancer cells are endowed of an altered redox state, usually characterized by high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and antioxidants (e.g., GSH). Regulating the redox homeostasis have been explored as a new strategy to treat cancer [3,4]. In particular, chemodynamic therapy (CDT), based on iron-mediated Fenton or Fenton-like reactions, can trigger intracellular oxidative stress by transforming endogenous hydrogen peroxide into highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (∙OH) [5–7]. CDT is rapidly developing as a more powerful modality than traditional therapies against many cancers [6]. Although a variety of CDT systems have been proposed, new approaches to increase its therapeutic value are still highly needed [5].

The overexpression of GSH in cancer cells is one of the most challenging obstacles to fully exploit CDT potential. Indeed the increase of GSH level provoke more cellular resistance to CDT [8]. GSH displays a potent scavenging effect on reactive hydroxyl radicals generated by chemodynamic molecules [8,9]. Thus, the depletion of intracellular GSH concentration is highly desirable for CDT agents to avoid tumor resistance and enahnce chemodynamic effectiveness. Cu2+-containing compounds are widely explored as CDT tools because they can efficiently deplete overexpressed GSH and converted endogenous H2O2 into highly active ∙OH [10–12]. During the CDT process, the generation of ∙OH depends on the catalytic transformation of endogenous H2O2 [13]. Generally, the amount of intracellular H2O2 in cancer cells is scarce to achieve satisfactory CDT outcome [14,15]. The excess of GSH in the tumor microenvironment (up to 10 mM) can reduce Cu (II) to Cu (I) by Fenton-like reaction, and Cu (I) further catalyzes H2O2 to harvest ∙OH [16]. Moreover, this Cu-related Fenton-like reaction can work in a wide pH range, with high efficiency in slightly acidic and neutral environments [10,17]. As a consequence, Cu2+-containing nanoagents have been viewed as promising CDT alternative to improve cancer therapeutic efficacy.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are a growing class of carbon materials with unique optical and electronic properties [18,19]. Recent literature has seen a blossom of studies of these materials in biomedicine because of their good biocompatibility [20,21]. In particular, CNDs displayed superior ability to deliver metals and then to regulate intracellular oxidative stress [22–24]. Recently, some reports have confirmed that CNDs are excellent carriers for Cu2+ delivery. The resulting Cu2+ nanoagents usually showed high metal loading and good dispersibility, leading to a highly chemodynamic activity for cancer treatment [25,26]. However, two bottlenecks still are the limiting factors that significantly affect the translation of CNDs in clinical uses. One factor is associated with the toxicity of free Cu2+. Free Cu2+ and unstable molecules delivered non-specifically as CDT agents, easily cause oxidative stress throughout the whole body. The other factor is the in situ catalytic activity. If Cu2+ ions are wrapped inside a nanoparticle, they will not react with GSH, seriously affecting their catalytic rate. To date, a rational design of NIR carbon nanodot-based Cu2+ intercellular delivery system with target ability, stable metals loading, good biocompatibility and stable NIR visualization is still a challenge.

Hence, we account the use of NIR CNDs as a supporting material to obtain highly stable and well-dispersed copper-based nanoparticles to targeted regulate intracellular oxidative stress (Fig. 1 ). In this work, NIR CNDs were chosen as the versatile support material. DNA aptamer was selected as a model targeting ligand to specifically recognize cancer cells. The clinical drug metal chelating agent, diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA), was used to chelate copper ions. DTPA is a very efficient chelating agent for metal ions, including copper. Due to its high stability in vivo, the DTPA complex Magnevist® is used clinically as a contrast agent in magnetic resonance imaging [27–29]. Both DNA aptamer and DTPA-Cu were covalently conjugated onto the surface of CNDs through a controlled amidation and click reaction. The resulting AptCND-Cu are highly monodispersed nanoparticles displaying good water dispersibility, stable NIR FL emission, excellent photostability, and sustained copper delivery. Upon receptor-mediated endocytosis, Cu2+ ions can react with GSH to produce Cu+, generating reactive hydroxyl radicals from endogenous H2O2. Dysregulation of oxidative homeostasis resulting from GSH reduction makes cancer cells more vulnerable to ∙OH formed via the Cu+-mediated Fenton-like process, assisting and enhanced CDT. Additionally, benefiting from their stable NIR fluorescence signals, AptCND-Cu nanoparticles can be well visualized using noninvasive fluorescence microscopy.

All chemicals were purchased commercially and used without further purification. All chemicals were analytical grade or with the highest purity available. Ultrapure water with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ was produced using a Milli-Q® Pure/Ultrapure Water Production system. The Gibco 1× PBS buffer without magnesium chloride and calcium chloride was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. The McCoy's 5A cell culture media was purchased from BIOAGRIO (Shanghai, China). 2,2’-(Ethylenedioxy)bis (ethylamine), 3-azido-1-propanamine and CuCl2 were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. 3-(4-Bromophenyl) propanoic acid, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC∙HCl), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. DNA aptamer Sgc8-DBCO (aptamer-DBCO) was purchased from Sangon Biotech. The aptamer sequences (5′ to 3′) of Sgc8-DBCO is DBCO-(C6)-ATCTAACTGCTGCGCCGCCGGGAAAATACTGTACGGTTAGA.

LC/MS analyses were performed on Thermo & ISQ EM. 1H NMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker DPX 300 spectrometer. The values of the peaks were obtained as ppm (δ) and referenced to the solvent. The multiplicity of the resonances is indicated as s (singlet), d (doublet), t (triplet), dd (doublet of doublet), and m (multiplet). Ultrafiltration separation was performed with ultrafiltration Spin Columns from Millipore. Centrifugation were performed on an Eppendorf centrifuge 5425R. The dialysis was done using MWCO 1 kDa membranes from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Milli-Q® water was used in all experiments. UV–Vis spectra were registered using a Cary 100 UV–Visible spectrophotometer from Agilent Technologies. Fluorescence steady-state spectra were recorded via a FluoroMax-4 (Horiba Jobin Yvon) spectrometer equipped with a swig xenon 450 W lamp. The FTIR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet™ iS50 FTIR Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific™). The morphology of the samples was analyzed by HRTEM (TALOS F200×, Thermofisher, USA). HAADF-STEM images were acquired on a TALOS F200×, (Thermofisher, USA). DLS and zeta potential measurements were performed with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, U.K.). XPS was done using a Thermo Scientific KAlpha X-ray spectrometer with a mono Xray source Al Kα excitation (1486 eV). All binding energies were calibrated to C1s peak located at 284.8 eV associated to sp
3 carbon. The fine structures of the photoelectron lines were treated using Casa XPS software. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP–MS) analyses were done on a NexlON 2000, PerkinElmer. Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was measured on an FLS 1000, Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., using a Hamamatsu Quantaurus-QY integrating sphere under an air-equilibrated condition and an empty quartz tube as a reference.

CNDs were prepared using a slightly modified method from a previous publication [30]. Briefly, CNDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of polythiophene phenylpropionic acid (PPA). Twenty mg of PPA were milled for 2 h at 200 rpm with a Rescht Planetary Ball Mill PM 100.

The milled PPA was then dispersed in 40 mL of NaOH solution (1 M). The mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min, transferred into an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, CNDs were collected through filtration using a 0.22 μm PTFE membrane (Millipore), three times. The filtrate was dialyzed (using a 1 kDa membrane) against distilled water for 2 days to remove NaOH. Following the lyophilization, a red powder was obtained (10 mg, 50% yield).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of the N3-(CH2)3–NH2 to CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: A suspension of CNDs (10 mg) in 1× PBS buffer (10 mL) was sonicated in an ice bath for 10 min. NHS (100 mg), EDC·HCl (160 mg), and triethylamine (240 μL) were subsequently added, and the mixture was agitated for 4 h. Then, a solution of 3-azido-1-propanamine (20 mg) in DMSO (0.5 mL) was added and the mixture was stirred for addtional 20 h at room temperature. The mixture was dialyzed (using a 1 kDa membrane) against distilled water for 3 days. Following the lyophilization, the red solid N3-CND was obtained (12 mg).

A suspension of N3-CND (10 mg) in 1× PBS buffer (10 mL) was sonicated in an ice bath for 10 min. NHS (100 mg), EDC·HCl (160 mg), and TEA (240 μL) were added to the mixture at room temperature. The suspension was stirred for 2 h. Afterward, a solution of tBu-DTPA-NH2 (30 mg) in DMSO (0.5 mL) was added, and the mixture was stirred for additional 20 h at room temperature. Unreacted tBu-DTPA-NH2 was removed by dialysis (using a 2 kDa membrane) against distilled water for 2 days. After lyophilization, a dark red solid (N3-CND-DTPA-tBu) was obtained (13 mg).

Ten mg of N3-CND-DTPA-tBu were added to 3 mL of Milli-Q® water to form a red suspension. After sonication for 10 min, 3 mL of trifluroacetic acid was added dropwise to the suspension in an ice bath. Then, the suspension was stirred at room temperature for 20 h. The suspension was dialyzed using a 1 kDa membrane against distilled water for 2 days. The suspension was lyophilized to obtain N3-CND-DTPA (8 mg).

Six hundreds μL of a CuCl2 aqueous solution (200 mM) were added to a suspension of N3-CND-DTPA (8 mg) in 3 portions. After sonication for 10 min, the solution was stirred for 2 h at room temperature. Free Cu was removed via centrifugation and washed with distilled water for 3 times. The suspension was lyophilized to obtain CND-DTPA-Cu (6.6 mg).

A suspension of CND-DTPA-Cu in 1 × PBS (1 mg mL−1, 1 mL) was sonicated for 10 min. Then 200 μL of Sgc8-DBCO (100 μM) were added to the mixture at room temperature. The suspension was shaken on a 37 °C incubator for 24 h. Unbound Sgc8-DBCO was removed using ultrafiltration centrifuge tubes (Millipore, 100 kD membrane). The residue was washed with distilled water for 5 times. The NanoDrop™ One was used to monitor the residual amount of Sgc8-DBCO in the filtrate. The residue in the ultrafiltration centrifuge tubes was subsequently collected in water. After lyophilization, the red solid AptCND-Cu was obtained (0.9 mg).

During the collection and washing to eliminate the unreacted aptamer, all supernatants were analyzed to calculate the amount of unconjugated Sgc8. Sgc8 concentrations were determined by NanoDrop™ One microvolume UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (TheramoFisher, USA). Sgc8 loading efficiency was calculated using the following formula: Loading efficiency = [(Mi-Mu)/Mi] × 100%, where Mu is the total amount of Sgc8 unconjugated aptamer and Mi is the total quantity of Sgc8 added initially to the synthesis.

One mL of AptCND-Cu (50 μg mL−1) was added to 0.5 mL of 0.6 M nitric acid. After heating for 1 h at 120 °C, the mixture was diluted in 10 mL of 1× PBS buffer. The Cu content was then measured using ICP–MS analyses on a NexlON 2000.

One hundred μL of a 50 μg mL−1 dispersion of AptCND-Cu and 100 μL of a 1 mM of Cy5 solution were exposed to 10 W LED light (Philips flashlight) for 100 min at room temperature. The distance between the sample and the LED light was 3 cm. The fluorescence signals were collected at different time points via a fluorescence spectrometer.

One hundred μL of a 50 μg mL−1 dispersion of AptCND-Cu were stored at room temperature for 3 days. At each selected time point (every xx h), the filtrate was collected using an ultrafiltration centrifuge tube (Millipore, 3 kD membrane). One mL of filtrate was added to 0.5 mL of 0.6 M nitric acid. After heating for 1 h at 120 °C, the mixture was diluted in 10 mL of Milli-Q® water. The Cu content was then measured using ICP–MS analyses.

AptCND-Cu (3 μg) was incubated in 10% FBS at 37 °C for 3 days. Finally, all specimens collected at different time points were visualized on a 1.5% agarose gel, and a molecular imager (Bio-Rad) was employed to measure the band density.

Methylene blue (MB) was used as an indicator to measure the generation of hydroxyl radicals. A PBS buffer solution (200 μL, pH = 7.4) containing AptCND-Cu (50 μg mL−1) was prepared and 20 μL of GSH (100 mM), 4 μL of MB (1 mg mL−1) and 30 μL of H2O2 (100 μM) were added. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 1.5 h, and the absorbance changes of MB were monitored at 665 nm. The following samples were also tested: 1) MB; 2) MB + H2O2; 3) MB + H2O2 + GSH; (AptCND-Cu = 50 μg mL−1, H2O2 = 11 μM, GSH = 8 mM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH and GSSG in solution
ce:section.ce:para: Two GSH aliquots (each of 10 μL, 1 mM) with or without AptCND-Cu (100 μL, 200 μg mL−1) were prepared. After the incubation at 37 °C for 1 h, the supernatant was extracted from the respective aliquots for GSSG and GSH content test using a GSSG/GSH assay kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of AptCND-Cu at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: AptCND-Cu (200 μL) at concentration of 50 μg mL−1 was split in three PBS buffer solutions at pH = 5, 6.5, 7.4, respectively. The three samples were added to 20 μL GSH in PBS (100 mM). After incubation at 37 °C for 15 min, 4 μL of MB (1 mg mL−1) and 30 μL of H2O2 (100 μM) were added. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min, and the absorbance changes of MB were monitored at 665 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of AptCND-Cu at different temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Twenty μL of GSH in PBS (100 mM) were added to the AptCND-Cu solution (200 μL) at concentration of 50 μg mL−1. After incubation at 37 °C for 15 min, 4 μL of MB (1 mg mL−1) and 30 μL of H2O2 (100 μM) were added. The sample was incubated at 25 °C, 37 °C, 45 °C for 30 min，respectively. The absorbance changes of MB were monitored at 665 nm.

Carboxylate-rich NIR CNDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method using polythiophene phenylpropionic acid as carbon source, as described in our previous work [30]. An azide derivative was first conjugated to the COOH groups of CNDs (Fig. 2 ). Considering the second coupling reaction with DTPA ligand, the amount of azide ligand was controlled to not saturate all the COOH groups of CNDs. Secondly, the DTPA ligand was conjugated to CNDs via a second amidation reaction. After removing the tert-butyl protecting groups of DTPA, we obtained the double modified N3-CND-DTPA. The DTPA on the surface of CNDs endow CNDs with metal chelating ability. Subsequently, N3-CND-DTPA-Cu was obtained by dispersing its precursor in a water solution of copper chloride followed by dialysis in Milli-Q® water to remove free Cu2+ ions. Finally, we prepared the aptamer-CND-DTPA-Cu (AptCND-Cu) through a copper-free click reaction. DNA aptamer could not only endow CNDs with targeting ability, but also enhanced their colloidal stability.

The synthetic process and the resulting conjugates were monitored and characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), FT-IR, zeta potential, and dynamic light scattering (DLS). As shown in HRTEM images the pristine CNDs own a size below 5 nm (Fig. 3 ). After modification with DTPA-Cu and DNA aptamer, the final AptCND-Cu displayed a bigger size maintaining their round edges. AptCND-Cu nanodots are uniform and monodispersed quasi-spherical nanoparticles with diameters below 10 nm. The high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) observations revealed that copper is distributed homogeneously on AptCND-Cu (Fig. 3b). Compared to CNDs, the FT-IR spectra of N3-CND-DTPA (Fig. 3c, and S1), allowed to identify the N–H and amide I stretching at 3296 and 1637 cm−1, respectively. The shoulder around 1720 cm−1 confirmed the presence of CO stretching of free carboxylic groups. The peak at 2100 cm−1 was indicative of the presence of the azide. From the zeta potential, we could see that the pristine CNDs own negative surface charge due to the carboxylic groups on their surface (Fig. 3d). The surface conjugation with DTPA, increased the negative charge value of CNDs from −10 to −30 mV, indicating that the attachment of DTPA can improve the colloidal stability of CNDs. Once conjugated with Cu, the charge value of the modified dots increased to −17 mV, but after clicking Sgc8 aptamer, the zeta potential decreased again to −40 mV, ensuring a better colloidal stability. DLS measurements (Fig. 3e) confirmed that doubly modified NIR CNDs with DNA aptamer and DTPA-Cu augmented their average hydrated particle size from 6 to 40 nm. The final AptCND-Cu nanoparticles displayed a hydrodynamic radius distribution ranging from 20 to 70 nm, consistent with our previous reported folic acid and Ce6 double-modified CNDs [31]. The size distribution of the nanoparticles assessed by DLS is usually larger than that obtained by other techniques like TEM, since DLS measures the hydrodynamic size of the core and it also take into account the surrounding solvation molecules [32,33].

From atomic force microscopy images (Fig. 4 a and b), AptCND-Cu displayed a height around 3–5 nm. Agarose gel electrophoresis further confirmed the efficient coupling between DNA aptamer and CND-DTPA-Cu. In Fig. 4c, the green band corresponds to DNA aptamer-DBCO, the red band is associated to N3-CND-DTPA-Cu, and the yellow band correspond to the merging of AptCND-Cu. The aptamer content onto CNDs was calculated through a reduction method, and corresponded to 16 μmol of oligomer per gram of nanoparticles. The typical peaks equivalent to C1s (284.9 eV), N1s (399.6 eV), O1s (532.2 eV), S2p (163.8 eV), P2p (133.4 eV), Cu2p (933.4 eV) were observed in the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 4d, Fig. S3), confirming that AptCND-Cu was primarily composed of these elements. The presence of the two Cu 2P1/2 (952.6 eV) and Cu 2P3/2 (932.9 eV) peaks supported the fact that the oxidation state of Cu in AptCND-Cu remains bivalent (Fig. 4e), which is consistent with previous works [34,35]. The copper content in AptCND-Cu was further measured by ICP-MS, and corresponded to 6.5% of Cu per gram of nanoparticles, that is higher than the percentage reported for Cu-CD-Ce6 nanoparticles (3.6%) [36]. Altogether, these results allowed us to conclude that we have successfully constructed the NIR CNDs-based DNA-Cu nanoparticles.

Following the chemical and morphological characterization of these multifunctional CNDs, we studied their optical properties and stability. AptCND-Cu displayed UV–Vis absorption bands from 200 to 700 nm (Fig. 5 a). Compared to the UV of CNDs precursor (Fig. S2), the additional peak observed at 260 nm is the characteristic peak of an aptamer. AptCND-Cu was characterized by a broad fluorescence emission spectrum from 500 to 800 nm (Fig. 5a, S4). The fluorescence quantum yield of AptCND-Cu was measured using a spectrophotometer attached to an integrating sphere and corresponded to 1.03% in PBS. This quantum yield (QY) is similar to the unmodified CNDs (QY = 0.9%), higher than that of CD nanoparticles (0.15%) and Se-doped CDs (0.2%) prepared from polythiophene derivatives [37,38]. Interestingly, we found that unmodified CNDs easily aggregated once incubated with Cu2+ and their fluorescence was quenched due to its strong electron-withdrawing capability of copper ions [26]. After being modified with the alkyl chain and DTPA, CND-Cu dispersed well in aqueous solution displaying good fluorescent properties. The QY of CND-Cu and N3-CND-DTPA was measured and corresponded to 1.1% and 2%, respectively. This indicates that non-aggregated state and a less effective electron transfer endow our nanoparticles with superior fluorescence performance in solutions. In addition, AptCND-Cu own superior photobleaching resistance as shown in Fig. 5b. The fluorescence signal remained still higher than 50% under a 10 W LED light exposure for 60 min, resulting more stable than commercially available near-infrared probe Cy5, which lost 99% of fluorescence signal after 60 min irradiation. The superior NIR emission and photostability of AptCND-Cu ensures a long-term visualization in complex physiological environment. In the recent years, many nanoparticles, including copper–DNAzyme nanohybrids [39], copper-doped calcium phosphate [40], core–shell cuprous oxide catalysts [41], and copper-based metal organic frameworks [42], have been explored for effective Cu delivery, achieving remarkable cancer chemodynamic therapeutic efficacy. However, carbon dot-based Cu nanoparticles, which own stable NIR emission were rarely explored [36]. Benefiting from their superior NIR emission and photostability, our AptCND-Cu nanosystem could achieve an efficient imaging-guided Cu ion delivery.

We then verified that AptCND-Cu had a superior dispersibility and stability in physiological solutions. The nanoparticles remained stable in a broad range of pH (e.g., 3–12). They were also stable in 10% FBS-containing cell medium for more than 72 h (Fig. 5c, Fig. S5). The stability of the aptamer was higher than that of free DNA Sgc8 whose serum stability was usually reported to be less than 4 h [43]. We did observe no aggregation phenomena in AptCND-Cu solution over weeks even at a high concentration (up to 1 mg mL−1). In the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 5a), we did not observe the peaks of Cu2+ expected at 814 nm, a sign that there was no free Cu2+ in solution [11]. To further confirm that Cu2+ remained stably chelated on the surface of CNDs, a 50 μg mL−1 AptCND-Cu solution was stored at room temperature for different times (0–72 h) (Fig. 5d). At each time point analyzed, the filtrate was collected after ultrafiltration for a copper quantitative analysis using ICP-MS. As shown in Fig. 5d, negligible Cu2+ leakage was observed up to 72 h. This result indicates that Cu2+ is strongly chelated to the surface of CNDs. DTPA is a chelating agent with five carboxylate sites and was grafted onto different types of nanoparticles, including carbon tubes [44], carbon dots [23], silk fibroin [45], gold nanoparticles [46], and silica nanoparticles [47], forming highly stable chelates with heavy metal for molecular imaging. DTPA-Cu owns a high stability constant at 15.4 [29], that prevents copper leakage during drug delivery. However, to the best of our knowledge, covalently modify DTPA-Cu onto NIR CNDs for cancer chemodynamic therapy has not been reported yet.

Next, we verified that AptCND-Cu could effectively convert reduced glutathione (GSH) into oxidized glutathione disulfide (GSSG) during the reaction process using a GSH/GSSG assay kit [48]. Firstly, 100 μL of a 2.5 μM solution of GSH was tested as control. As shown in Fig. 6 a, no GSSG was detected in this control sample. After co-incubation of AptCND-Cu and GSH (2.5 μM) for 1 h at 37 °C, 1.2 μmol of GSSG were measured in the sample. Because GSSG is formed by the dehydrogenation of two GSH molecules, this means that over 95% of GSH was converted into GSSG (Fig. 6a), indicating GSH consumption during the generation of ∙OH. This result is consistent with previous data reported in the literature [49]. Furthermore, we evaluated the ability of AptCND-Cu to produce hydroxyl radicals via Fenton-like reaction by using methylene blue (MB) as an indicator. After incubation of AptCND-Cu with H2O2 and GSH for 90 min in the presence of MB, the absorbance of MB significantly decreased (Fig. 6b), while, the control samples without AptCND-Cu did not change the UV spectra of MB. Collectively, these results demonstrate that AptCND-Cu could efficiently generate ∙OH with the assistance of GSH and H2O2. We also found that the catalytic activity of the nanoparticles increased with the increase of the temperature from 25 °C to 45 °C (Fig. 6c). We then measured MB degradation reaction rates at different pH values: representing the conditions in lysosomes/endosomes (pH 5.0), the conditions in tumor microenvironment (pH 6.5), and the physiological conditions (pH 7.4). We noticed only a slight difference in the spectra intensity at the different pH (Fig. 6d). This property would ensure the high efficiency of the AptCND-Cu mediated catalytic in situ therapy in tumor tissues. Thus, the depletion of GSH and the generation of ∙OH provides a practical basis for efficient cancer therapy.

We next investigated the targeted cellular uptake and the cell fluorescence imaging of AptCND-Cu in the HCT 116 human colon cancer cell line. PTK7 protein, a transmembrane receptor, is frequently overexpressed on the surface of cancer cells. DNA aptamer Sgc8 composed of 41 bases can specifically bind to PTK7 with a binding force at the nM level. Sgc8 has been widely used as a targeting ligand for the delivery of therapeutics [43,50]. To evaluate the specific cell targeting of AptCND-Cu, flow cytometry analysis was used to meassure its binding ability to HCT 116 cells. Following incubation with HCT 116 cells for 30 min, AptCND-Cu could effectively bind to HCT 116 cells (Fig. S6). The specificity of Sgc8-PTK7 interaction was further demonstrated by the inhibition of AptCND-Cu binding upon addition of an excess of Sgc8 as a binding competitor during incubation with HCT 116 cells (Fig. 7 a and b). Moreover, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was used to monitor the FL signals of the different nanoparticles. HCT 116 cells were treated with AptCND-Cu, AptCND, CNDs at different time points (0, 4, 8, 24 h). CLSM revealed that both AptCND-Cu and AptCND can penetrate cancer cells within 4 h displaying strong NIR fluorescence signals (665–750 nm) (Figs. 7c, S7). The fluorescence signals clearly increased at long time incubation. Pristine CNDs were instead observed mainly outside cancer cells, even after 24 h of incubation. This is in agreement with our previous studies [30]. These data indicated that both AptCND-Cu and AptCND are able to efficiently penetrate tumor cells without the help of transfecting reagents. To further identify the actual location of AptCND-Cu at subcellular level, we tested the co-localization of AptCND-Cu with mitochondria, lysosomes, and nucleus using proper dyes. The CLAM images show that our nanoparticles co-localize with lysosomes (PCC = 0.48) and mitochondria (PCC = 0.11). However, they were not observed in nucleus (Fig. S8). Compared with CNDs that were easily aggregated outside the cells, any aggregation of AptCND-Cu was observed during the imaging experiment, confirming the good biodispersibility and biocompatibility of our dots. Moreover, stable NIR FL in cancer cells make these nanoparticles as excellent carriers for visualized copper ion delivery.

After proving the targeted cell uptake of our AptCND-Cu, we examined the intracellular activity of generated ∙OH. 2,7-Dichlorofluorescein-diacetate (DCFH-DA) was used as the fluorogenic marker for ROS detection. CNDs, AptCND and AptCND-Cu nanoparticles at a concentration of 100 μg mL−1 were co-incubated with HCT 116 cancer cells for 8 h. After staining with DCFH-DA probe, the FL signals were collected. As shown in Fig. 8 , the HCT 116 cells incubated with AptCND-Cu presented much stronger green fluorescence than the control group, suggesting a higher production of ROS. The control CNDs and AptCND samples did not cause any ROS production. This result revealed that AptCND-Cu can delivery copper ions into cancer cell and trigger the production of high levels of intracellular ∙OH. All data together confirmed the mechanism for radical generation, which could be used for catalytic tumor therapy.

Notably, as shown in the bright field of cell images (Fig. 7), we found that once incubated with AptCND-Cu, the cell borders became round and cell adhesion decreased, signs of cancer cell death. Based on this observation, we next studied the effects of the catalytic therapy of AptCND-Cu in vitro. Calcein AM/propidium iodide (PI) assay kit was used to differentiate live and dead HCT 116 cells. As shown in Fig. 9 a, we found that in the CNDs treated group, all cells displayed strong green fluorescence, meaning that they remained alive. In the AptCND-Cu (100 μg mL−1) treated group, the cell density significantly decreased and many cancer cells resulted dead, as indicated by the homogeneous red fluorescence. Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay was further used to evaluated the cytotoxic impact of CNDs, AptCND and AptCND-Cu (Fig. 9b). The high cell viability of CNDs and AptCND treated groups confirmed again their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. In AptCND-Cu treated group, the percentage of live cancer cells diminished in a dose dependent manner. These results indicated that AptCND-Cu can efficiently destroy cancer cells.

Finally, we assessed the targeting imaging and biodistribution of the nanoparticles in the vital organs for in vivo biomedical applications. AptCND-Cu and CNDs (200 μL, 1 mg mL−1) were intravenously (i.v.) injected into HCT 116 bearing nude mice. Their fluorescent images were acquired at 0, 2 min, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 5 and 24 h. As shown in Fig. 10 a and b, NIR signals were observed in the whole body of mice within 2 min, indicating a quick systemic diffusion of AptCND-Cu nanoparticles. The fluorescence signals of AptCND-Cu in tumor lesions increased sharply from 0.5 h, reaching higher intensities than that of CNDs in mice control groups. After 24 h, NIR signals of AptCND-Cu supported the presence of the carbon nanodots into the tumor. An accumulation was also observed in the liver and bladder, which is indicative of a hepatobiliary and renal urinary dual elimination route. The ex vivo fluorescence signals (Fig. 10c and f) in liver and kidney confirmed the two elimination pathways of AptCND-Cu. These signals in major organs decreased with time, suggesting the relatively fast clearance of AptCND-Cu and their safety in vivo (Fig. 10d, e, 10g and 10h).

In summary, we have developed a flexible strategy for a double-covalent modification of NIR CNDs, obtaining NIR carbon dot-based oxidative stress amplifier for targeted cell imaging and intracellular production of ROS. The conjugation is based on a controlled amidation procedure followed by a click reaction. The morphology and structure of the modified dots were assessed by the combination of various techniques. The resulting AptCND-Cu nanoparticles owns targeting ability, stable NIR FL emission, good photostability, and stable copper delivery. These nanoparticles can deplete intracellular GSH, amplify Cu-induced Fenton-like reaction, and finally causing cell death in vitro. Taken together, these results show that AptCND-Cu is a promising anticancer agent by targeting redox homeostasis in cancer cells. In vivo tests indicate that AptCND-Cu owns a long a long tumor residence time and effective body clearance through a hepatobiliary and renal urinary dual elimination route.

Our reported strategy is a versatile method to prepare doubly covalent functionalized NIR CNDs as an efficient ion delivery carrier for catalytic cancer therapy. Considering the broad DNA aptamer library, different kinds of tumor-targeting catalytic drugs can be produced by easily changing the targeting DNA aptamer ligands. Furthermore, since DTPA can form complexes with different transition metals, our AptCND nanocarrier could be easily exploited for the preparation of other types of metal-chelated drugs, including iron neodymium, manganese, europium or gallium.
